stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a1]] & [[b1]] \\ [[a2]] & [[b2]] \end{bmatrix} \) and \( \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} [[c1]] \\ [[c2]] \end{bmatrix} \). Since \( A \) is invertible, the equation \( A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b} \) has the unique solution \( \mathbf{x} = A^{-1}\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} \). What is \( x_1 \)?
solution: Computing \( A^{-1}\mathbf{b} \) (equivalently, solving the two scalar equations) gives \( x_1 = [[answer]] \), with \( x_2 = [[q]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the second component \( x_2 \), not \( x_1 \).
This has the right magnitude but the wrong sign.
This adds the two components together.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a1]] & [[b1]] & [[c1]] \\ [[a2]] & [[b2]] & [[c2]] \\ [[a3]] & [[b3]] & [[c3]] \end{bmatrix} \) and \( \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} [[d1]] \\ [[d2]] \\ [[d3]] \end{bmatrix} \). Since \( \det(A) \ne 0 \), \( A \) has an inverse (built from its \( 3\times3 \) adjugate), and \( A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b} \) has the unique solution \( \mathbf{x}=A^{-1}\mathbf{b} \). What is the first component \( x_1 \)?
solution: Computing \( A^{-1}\mathbf{b} \) via the adjugate matrix (or solving the three scalar equations directly) gives \( x_1 = [[answer]] \) (with \( x_2=[[q]] \), \( x_3=[[t]] \)).
distractor_rationales: This is \( x_2 \), not \( x_1 \).
This is \( x_3 \), not \( x_1 \).
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: The matrix \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b1]] \\ [[ka]] & [[kb1]] \end{bmatrix} \) has no inverse (its second row is [[k]] times its first). Even so, the system \( A\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} [[r1]] \\ [[kr1]] \end{bmatrix} \) is consistent, since the right-hand side is also [[k]] times the first equation's value; it has infinitely many solutions. What is the value of \( x_1 \) in the particular solution with \( x_2 = 0 \)?
solution: Setting \( x_2=0 \) in the first row's equation \( [[a]]x_1 + ([[b1]])x_2 = [[r1]] \) gives \( [[a]]x_1 = [[r1]] \), so \( x_1 = [[answer]] \). (The second row, being [[k]] times the first, is automatically satisfied too, confirming consistency.)
distractor_rationales: This is the coefficient \( a \) itself, not the solved value \( x_1 \).
This is the coefficient \( b_1 \), unrelated to \( x_1 \) once \( x_2=0 \).
This is the row-scaling factor \( k \), not \( x_1 \).
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a1]] & [[b1]] \\ [[a2]] & [[b2]] \end{bmatrix} \) and \( \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} [[c1]] \\ [[c2]] \end{bmatrix} \). Since \( A \) is invertible, \( A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b} \) has the unique solution \( \mathbf{x}=A^{-1}\mathbf{b}=\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} \). What is \( x_1 + x_2 \)?
solution: Computing \( A^{-1}\mathbf{b} \) gives \( x_1=[[p]] \) and \( x_2=[[q]] \), so \( x_1+x_2=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( x_1 \) alone, not the sum \( x_1+x_2 \).
This is \( x_2 \) alone, not the sum \( x_1+x_2 \).
This is the DIFFERENCE \( x_1-x_2 \), not the sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by two from the correct sum.
This is off by two from the correct sum.
